subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 3 periods = 120 minutes
Total: 3 periods · 2h 0min

periods.activity_title: Reading a Prayer-Poem: Tone, Theme, and Key Lines

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-reading: which sentence is closest to a prayer?
Read poem, underline agreed lines, share in groups
Circle correct words to complete the poem summary

phases.description: Teacher writes the three pre-reading sentences on the board and asks students which one reads like a prayer, prompting discussion on tone and intent. Students explain their choice to classmates, setting up the poem's devotional register.

Students read the poem silently, underlining lines they agree with most. In groups of three they share which lines they chose and explain their reasons; the group identifies the line most commonly underlined and reports it to the class.

Teacher guides students through the summary-completion task on page 54, circling the correct word from each pair (e.g., 'prayer for / speech on', 'complete life / school days'). Students explain why the incorrect option does not fit the poem's meaning.
Quick close: teacher asks — 'What makes this poem different from a simple wish?' Students offer one phrase each. Teacher previews the passage-extract questions for independent reading.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition from Section A; this poem section is much shorter (380 characters) than the narrative, so one RFC period is appropriate. The poem_appreciation_summary notes the AABB rhyme scheme and the devotional tone — draw attention to this when students discuss which lines feel like a prayer. Task_index 1, 4–10 are left as self-study.

periods.activity_title: Outdoor Friends: Listening and Role-Play Conversations

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Answer one-two word questions about Ajay and Dhiraj's activities
Circle pictures matching activities from listening passage
Role-play two friend situations: park walk and tiffin sharing

phases.description: Teacher sets context: 'We heard about an unlikely friendship last time; now listen to two real friends — Ajay and Dhiraj — describing their favourite activities together.' Students listen to the passage and answer the one-or-two-word question about what the friends purchase at the park bench (page 56).
Teacher reads the passage a second time; students circle the correct pictures from the six shown on page 56, then compare with a partner. Class confirms answers together.

Teacher models the role-play framework for Situation 1 (a walk in the park): greeting, invitation, planning activities, and expressing preference. One pair demonstrates; teacher coaches the register and politeness markers.

Students work in pairs on both situations — the park walk and the tiffin-sharing conversation — taking turns in each role. Teacher circulates and cues quieter students with the printed sentence starters.

Two pairs perform a short role-play for the class; peers give feedback on one thing done well. Teacher connects the friendship values in the role-plays back to the poem's idea of 'giving one's best' for a friend.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition from Section B's RFC period; listening and speaking are combined per NCF guidance. The transcript on page 72 should be read twice at a natural pace. Section B's writing task is left as self-study — point students to the 80-word paragraph prompt (page 56) for independent completion.

periods.activity_title: Synonyms for Blessed, Friend Qualities, and Friendship Quotations

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Select synonyms for 'blessed' from given word list
Complete partial words for friend qualities; fill five sentences
Interpret two friendship quotations; share matching life experience

homework.task_brief: Let us explore (p.58): Find, decorate and display a friendship quotation on class board

phases.description: Teacher highlights the line 'With special friends I feel I'm blessed' from the poem and asks students to select synonyms from the given list on page 55 (grateful, thankful, etc.), discussing why 'excited' does not fit the poem's devotional tone.

Students decode the partially spelled qualities of a good friend (loyal, h-e-l-p-f-u-l, k-i-n-d, c-a-r-i-n-g, h-o-n-e-s-t) and then fill in the five sentences on pages 55–56, using each quality word in context.

Teacher reads the two friendship quotations on page 58 aloud. Pairs discuss the meaning of each and then share a personal experience that matches one quotation. Three or four pairs share with the whole class.

Teacher sets the homework: find a famous friendship quotation in any language, write it neatly, colour and decorate it, and bring it for the class display board. Section B's writing task (80-word paragraph) is recommended as an additional self-study exercise.

periods.teacher_notes: Vocabulary_grammar and beyond_text are combined here because the poem section is short and the vocabulary tasks are brief; both spines can be fully covered within the 40 minutes. The poem_appreciation_summary notes that 'blessed' carries a sense of gratitude and devotion — use that nuance when discussing the synonym selection task.

items.source_lo: Student identifies tone, central idea, and key imagery in a lyric poem.
items.item_stem:
Read the following stanza from 'A Friend's Prayer' by Jill Wolf:

'Let me use my heart to see,
To realise what friends can be,
And make no judgements from afar,
But love my friends the way they are.'

Which of the following best describes the central idea of these lines?

options.label: A
options.text: True friendship requires giving expensive gifts to friends.

options.label: B
options.text: Empathy and unconditional acceptance are the foundations of genuine friendship.

options.label: C
options.text: A friend should judge others carefully before trusting them.

options.label: D
options.text: The heart is less important than the mind in understanding friendship.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Students who choose this conflate friendship with material generosity; use the stanza to show the poem is about emotional attunement, not gifts.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Reverses the poem's message — the poet asks for openness, not careful judgement before trust; this reveals a more transactional view of friendship.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Suggests the student has read 'heart' metaphorically but then dismissed it; revisit the stanza to show the heart is positioned as the instrument of understanding.

items.question_text:
Read the following stanza from 'A Friend's Prayer' by Jill Wolf:

'Let me use my heart to see,
To realise what friends can be,
And make no judgements from afar,
But love my friends the way they are.'

Which of the following best describes the central idea of these lines?

items.source_lo: Student identifies specific activities and details from a short spoken description.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to a short talk about the outdoor activities that two friends, Ajay and Dhiraj, enjoy together. After listening, fill in the blanks with ONE or TWO words from the talk.

1. On some days, Ajay and Dhiraj spend time at the _____________ in the park.
2. Ajay's favourite activity at the park is the _____________.
3. Both friends go _____________ around the neighbourhood on Saturdays.
4. The one activity both friends truly enjoy is eating _____________ together.
items.question_text:
You will listen to a short talk about the outdoor activities that two friends, Ajay and Dhiraj, enjoy together. After listening, fill in the blanks with ONE or TWO words from the talk.

1. On some days, Ajay and Dhiraj spend time at the _____________ in the park.
2. Ajay's favourite activity at the park is the _____________.
3. Both friends go _____________ around the neighbourhood on Saturdays.
4. The one activity both friends truly enjoy is eating _____________ together.

items.source_lo: Student sustains a role-play conversation about shared activities with a partner.
items.item_stem: With a partner, role-play the following situation: You and your friend have just finished school and are deciding how to spend Saturday afternoon together. One of you wants to go to the park; the other prefers to stay in and read. Greet each other, share your preferences, listen to your partner's idea, suggest a compromise, and close the conversation politely. Your conversation should be at least 8 exchanges long.

expected_elements: Opens with a natural greeting and states preference clearly.
Listens and responds to partner's suggestion without interrupting.
Maintains polite register throughout; closes conversation naturally.

items.question_text: With a partner, role-play the following situation: You and your friend have just finished school and are deciding how to spend Saturday afternoon together. One of you wants to go to the park; the other prefers to stay in and read. Greet each other, share your preferences, listen to your partner's idea, suggest a compromise, and close the conversation politely. Your conversation should be at least 8 exchanges long.

items.source_lo: Student identifies synonyms and vocabulary for positive qualities of friendship.
items.item_stem: The poet Jill Wolf uses the word 'blessed' to describe how special friends make her feel. Match each word in Column A with its closest meaning from Column B. All the words in Column A are qualities of a good friend.
items.visual_stimulus:
Column A|Column B
1. loyal|(a) always tells the truth and does not deceive
2. caring|(b) gives support and makes things easier for others
3. honest|(c) shows warmth and concern for the feelings of others
4. kind|(d) stays devoted and does not betray
5. helpful|(e) gentle and considerate in treatment of others

answer_key.right: stays devoted and does not betray
shows warmth and concern for the feelings of others
always tells the truth and does not deceive
gentle and considerate in treatment of others
gives support and makes things easier for others

items.question_text: The poet Jill Wolf uses the word 'blessed' to describe how special friends make her feel. Match each word in Column A with its closest meaning from Column B. All the words in Column A are qualities of a good friend.

items.source_lo: Student interprets friendship quotations and connects them to personal experience.
items.item_stem: In 'A Friend's Prayer', the poet writes: 'I want to do much more than share / The hopes and plans of friends who care.' This expresses the idea that a true friend gives more than they take. Consider the following well-known saying about friendship: 'A friend in need is a friend indeed.' In 4–5 sentences, explain what you think this saying means and describe a real or imagined situation in which you saw this quality of friendship in action.

expected_elements: States the meaning of the quotation clearly in own words.
Gives a specific situation (real or imagined) that illustrates the saying.
Connects the situation to the quality of genuine care or support.
Links the quotation's meaning to the poem's theme of selfless friendship.

items.question_text: In 'A Friend's Prayer', the poet writes: 'I want to do much more than share / The hopes and plans of friends who care.' This expresses the idea that a true friend gives more than they take. Consider the following well-known saying about friendship: 'A friend in need is a friend indeed.' In 4–5 sentences, explain what you think this saying means and describe a real or imagined situation in which you saw this quality of friendship in action.
